title.en: Landing Zones & the Cloud Adoption Framework

description.en: Assemble everything the track has taught into the Azure landing zone architecture: CAF's methodology, the platform/application split, design areas, subscription vending, and how greenfield and brownfield estates adopt it.

story_variants.en:
## The Payoff Lesson

Every previous lesson built a component: management groups, RBAC, Bicep, hub-spoke, Key Vault, Policy. A **landing zone** is what happens when you assemble them into a factory — an environment where a new workload can land with governance, networking, identity, and monitoring *already in place*. Microsoft's **Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF)** is the methodology around that factory; the **Azure landing zone (ALZ) architecture** is its reference implementation. When a consultant says 'enterprise-scale', this is what they mean.

## CAF in One Paragraph

CAF sequences cloud adoption into disciplines: **Strategy** (why), **Plan** (what and who), **Ready** (build the landing zones — this lesson), **Adopt** (migrate/innovate), then the continuous pair **Govern** and **Manage**, with **Secure** woven throughout. Enterprises that skip Ready and jump to Adopt build twenty bespoke snowflake environments, then spend a painful year converging them — the framework exists because that failure mode is *that* common.

## The Architecture: Platform vs Application

The load-bearing idea is the split between **platform landing zones** (shared services run by the platform team) and **application landing zones** (one per workload, vended to app teams). It maps directly onto the management-group tree from lesson one:

| Management group | Subscriptions | Contains |
|---|---|---|
| Platform / Identity | Identity sub | Domain controllers, Entra sync |
| Platform / Connectivity | Connectivity sub | Hub VNet, firewall, gateways, private DNS zones |
| Platform / Management | Management sub | Log Analytics, automation, dashboards |
| Landing zones / Corp | One sub per internal workload | Spokes with no internet ingress |
| Landing zones / Online | One sub per public-facing workload | Spokes with managed ingress |
| Sandbox | Per-team subs | Loose policies, budget caps, no peering |
| Decommissioned | Retiring subs | Deny-all policy |

Every design decision you have already learned slots in: ZRS and lifecycle policies in the workload subs, policy initiatives assigned at `landing-zones`, DINE deploying diagnostics to the management sub's Log Analytics workspace, spokes peered to the connectivity sub's hub.

## Design Areas: The Checklist

ALZ formalizes eight design areas — treat them as the review agenda for any new estate: **billing and tenant**, **identity and access**, **management groups and subscriptions**, **network topology and connectivity**, **security**, **management (monitoring/ops)**, **governance**, and **platform automation and DevOps**. None of them is optional; the ordering is roughly the order this track taught them, which is not a coincidence.

## Subscription Vending: The Factory Door

The practical heartbeat of a mature landing zone is **subscription vending**: a pipeline (Bicep `lz-vending` modules or Terraform equivalents) that, on an approved request, creates the subscription, places it under the right management group, stamps the spoke VNet and peering, assigns the workload team's RBAC group, applies tags and budgets, and registers it in the CMDB — in minutes, without a human touching the portal. This converts platform engineering from ticket-driven toil into a product with an API.

> **War story:** A European insurer measured 'time to first deployable environment' at 34 days: security review, network request, firewall change board, manual subscription setup. After building ALZ with vending pipelines, the same request took 40 minutes, because every review board's requirements were *encoded in the platform* — policy assignments, baked-in spokes, mandatory diagnostics. The boards did not disappear; they moved from reviewing every workload to reviewing the factory once per quarter. That reframing — govern the template, not the instances — is the entire economic argument for landing zones.

## Greenfield, Brownfield, and the Accelerators

- **Greenfield**: deploy the **ALZ accelerator** (portal-driven) or the Bicep/Terraform ALZ modules (`Azure/ALZ-Bicep`, the `alz` Terraform provider ecosystem) and customize. Do not hand-build what the accelerator stamps out.
- **Brownfield**: you rarely get to start over. The playbook: create the target management-group tree beside the existing chaos, assign policies in audit mode, then *migrate subscriptions* into the tree group by group, graduating policies to enforce as each cohort complies. Subscription moves are instant governance events (lesson one), which makes this incremental path tractable.
- **Don't over-adopt**: a 30-person company does not need the full corp/online split on day one. ALZ scales down — start with platform + landing-zones + sandbox and grow. The anti-pattern is not smallness; it is bespokeness.

## Common Pitfalls

- **Snowflake landing zones**: every workload negotiates custom guardrails, and the 'factory' becomes a boutique.
- **Platform team as bottleneck**: vending must be self-service with approval gates, or teams route around it back to shadow IT.
- **Frozen accelerator**: deploying ALZ once and never pulling upstream policy/module updates — the reference architecture evolves continuously.
- **Skipping the management sub**: without central Log Analytics from day one, the observability lesson's cross-workload queries have nothing to query.
- **Org-chart management groups** (again): archetypes, not departments.

## Mental Model

A landing zone is **a paved road with guardrails, not a parking garage**: workloads drive themselves, fast, in their own lanes — but the lanes were engineered once, centrally, with the crash barriers built in. CAF tells you how to plan the road network; ALZ is the civil-engineering standard; subscription vending is the on-ramp. Everything the final two lessons add — cost visibility and observability — are the road's instrumentation, and they bolt onto this chassis.

description.en: Platform landing zones host shared services once; application landing zones are vended per workload with guardrails inherited.

left.label: 34 days of review boards per workload environment

right.label: 40-minute vending pipeline — requirements encoded in the platform

description.en: Govern the template once instead of reviewing every instance: the economic argument for landing zones.

explanation.en: One vending call creates the subscription with the standard naming scheme.
Placement under the corp management group is what applies every inherited policy instantly.
The spoke VNet comes from the central IPAM plan and is peered to the connectivity hub automatically.

lines: [OK] budget + tags stamped, RBAC group assigned

description.en: A workload environment born fully governed in under forty minutes — the landing-zone factory at work.
The CAF journey — with Ready as the phase this whole track has been preparing you to lead.

instruction.en: Build a landing-zone compliance scorecard. Each subscription dict has 'policy_assigned' (bool), 'diagnostics_enabled' (bool), and 'public_ips' (int). The three baseline checks, in this order, are: 'policy_assigned' (value is True), 'diagnostics_enabled' (value is True), 'no_public_ip' (public_ips == 0). Implement score(sub) returning (passed_count, [names of failed checks in order]). Print per subscription: '<name>: <passed>/3 PASS' when all pass, otherwise '<name>: <passed>/3 FAIL (<failed names joined by ", ">)'. Exactly this kind of conformance report is what platform teams publish for their landing zones.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
SUBSCRIPTIONS = [
    {'name': 'sub-prod', 'policy_assigned': True, 'diagnostics_enabled': True, 'public_ips': 0},
    {'name': 'sub-dev', 'policy_assigned': True, 'diagnostics_enabled': False, 'public_ips': 2},
]

def score(sub):
    # TODO: evaluate the three checks in order; return
    # (passed_count, list_of_failed_check_names)
    pass

# TODO: loop SUBSCRIPTIONS; print '<name>: <n>/3 PASS' or
# '<name>: <n>/3 FAIL (<failed, comma-joined>)'

en: Model the checks as ordered (name, boolean) pairs: [('policy_assigned', sub['policy_assigned']), ('diagnostics_enabled', ...), ('no_public_ip', sub['public_ips'] == 0)].
passed = number of True values; failed = the names where the value is False, preserving order.
Join failures with ', ' (comma + space): ', '.join(failed) — and only print the parenthetical on FAIL lines.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert score({'name': 'a', 'policy_assigned': True, 'diagnostics_enabled': True, 'public_ips': 0}) == (3, [])
assert score({'name': 'b', 'policy_assigned': True, 'diagnostics_enabled': False, 'public_ips': 2}) == (1, ['diagnostics_enabled', 'no_public_ip'])
assert score({'name': 'c', 'policy_assigned': False, 'diagnostics_enabled': True, 'public_ips': 0}) == (2, ['policy_assigned'])
assert score({'name': 'd', 'policy_assigned': False, 'diagnostics_enabled': False, 'public_ips': 1}) == (0, ['policy_assigned', 'diagnostics_enabled', 'no_public_ip'])

q.en: What distinguishes a platform landing zone from an application landing zone?

en: Platform zones host shared services (identity, connectivity, management) run centrally; application zones are vended per workload
Platform zones exist only in the primary region

q.en: In CAF, the 'Ready' phase is where you...

en: Build the landing zones before workloads arrive

q.en: The recommended brownfield path to ALZ is...

en: Build the target management-group tree, audit policies first, then migrate subscriptions cohort by cohort
Apply deny policies to the existing tree immediately

q.en: Subscription vending primarily converts platform engineering into...

en: A self-service product: governed environments created by pipeline in minutes
